title: Auth & Security
description: Authentication, token storage, and security best practices

references.title: Android Developers вҖ” Security Best Practices

lesson:
Authentication Patterns вҖ” OAuth 2.0 / OIDC

Most Android apps authenticate via OAuth 2.0 with the Authorization Code + PKCE flow. Understanding the flow and token types is essential:

OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow (with PKCE): в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ Android App Auth Server Resource API в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 1. в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Open browser в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (+ code_challenge) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 2. в”Ӯ User logs in в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ User consents в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 3. в”Ӯв—Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Redirect with в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ authorization CODE в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 4. в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Exchange code в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (+ code_verifier) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 5. в”Ӯв—Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Access + Refresh в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ tokens в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 6. в”Ӯв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ API call в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–¶в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (Authorization: Bearer access_token) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ 7. в”Ӯв—Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Response в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ӯ
PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange): Mobile apps cannot keep a client_secret secure (APK can be decompiled). PKCE replaces the secret with a one-time code_verifier + code_challenge pair. The verifier proves that the app requesting the token is the same one that started the flow. Always use PKCE for mobile OAuth.

Token types and their roles:

TokenLifetimePurposeStorage
Access TokenMinutes to hoursAuthorize API requests (Bearer header)Memory or EncryptedSharedPrefs
Refresh TokenDays to monthsGet new access tokens without re-loginEncryptedSharedPreferences
ID Token (OIDC)MinutesContains user identity claims (name, email) вҖ” JWT formatMemory only (don't persist)

Token Storage вҖ” EncryptedSharedPreferences

Plain SharedPreferences stores data as XML in /data/data/pkg/shared_prefs/ вҖ” readable on rooted devices. Always use EncryptedSharedPreferences for tokens.

// BAD: Plain text вҖ” readable on rooted devices, in backups val prefs = context.getSharedPreferences("auth", MODE_PRIVATE) prefs.edit().putString("token", token).apply() // GOOD: Encrypted with Android Keystore-backed key val masterKey = MasterKey.Builder(context) .setKeyScheme(MasterKey.KeyScheme.AES256_GCM) .build() val encryptedPrefs = EncryptedSharedPreferences.create( context, "secure_auth_prefs", masterKey, EncryptedSharedPreferences.PrefKeyEncryptionScheme.AES256_SIV, // keys encrypted EncryptedSharedPreferences.PrefValueEncryptionScheme.AES256_GCM // values encrypted ) // Usage is identical to regular SharedPreferences encryptedPrefs.edit().putString("refresh_token", token).apply() val token = encryptedPrefs.getString("refresh_token", null)
Android Keystore Architecture: в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ Your App в”Ӯ в”Ӯ EncryptedSharedPreferences в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в–ј в”Ӯ в”Ӯ MasterKey (reference) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в”Ӯ в–ј в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ӯ Android Keystore System в”Ӯ в”Ӯ (hardware-backed on most в”Ӯ в”Ӯ devices вҖ” TEE or StrongBox) в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ӯ AES-256 key material в”Ӯ в”Ӯ NEVER leaves secure hardware в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ Key point: The encryption key is stored in hardware. Even with root access, the key material cannot be extracted.

Network Security

Certificate Pinning

Certificate pinning ensures your app only communicates with servers presenting a specific certificate, not just any CA-signed cert. This defends against MITM attacks with rogue CAs.

// OkHttp CertificatePinner вҖ” pin by public key hash val client = OkHttpClient.Builder() .certificatePinner( CertificatePinner.Builder() .add( "api.myapp.com", "sha256/AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA=", // primary "sha256/BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB=" // backup ) .build() ) .build() // If the server's cert doesn't match вҶ’ connection fails // Protects against: rogue CAs, compromised CAs, corp proxy MITM

Network Security Configuration

Android's network_security_config.xml gives declarative control over network security policy:

// res/xml/network_security_config.xml <network-security-config> <!-- Block all cleartext (HTTP) traffic --> <base-config cleartextTrafficPermitted="false"> <trust-anchors> <certificates src="system" /> </trust-anchors> </base-config> <!-- Pin certificates for your API domain --> <domain-config> <domain includeSubdomains="true">api.myapp.com</domain> <pin-set expiration="2025-12-31"> <pin digest="SHA-256">base64encodedSHA256=</pin> <pin digest="SHA-256">backupPinBase64=</pin> </pin-set> </domain-config> <!-- Allow debug CAs for testing (debug builds only) --> <debug-overrides> <trust-anchors> <certificates src="user" /> </trust-anchors> </debug-overrides> </network-security-config> <!-- AndroidManifest.xml reference --> <application android:networkSecurityConfig="@xml/network_security_config">
Certificate pinning tradeoff: If your server rotates certificates and you don't update the app, ALL users are locked out. Always pin at least two keys (primary + backup), set an expiration date, and have a mechanism to update pins via remote config.

ProGuard / R8 вҖ” Code Obfuscation

R8 (the successor to ProGuard) is Android's code shrinker, optimizer, and obfuscator. It runs during release builds.

R8 does THREE things: в”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғв”Ғ 1. SHRINK вҶ’ Removes unused classes, methods, fields (Tree shaking вҖ” dead code elimination) 2. OPTIMIZE вҶ’ Inlines methods, simplifies code, removes dead branches 3. OBFUSCATE вҶ’ Renames classes/methods to a, b, c... Makes decompiled code unreadable Input: com.myapp.data.UserRepository.fetchUser() Output: a.b.c.a()
// build.gradle.kts вҖ” enable R8 for release builds android { buildTypes { release { isMinifyEnabled = true // enable R8 isShrinkResources = true // remove unused resources too proguardFiles( getDefaultProguardFile("proguard-android-optimize.txt"), "proguard-rules.pro" ) } } } // proguard-rules.pro вҖ” keep rules for things R8 must NOT touch // Keep data classes used with Gson/Moshi (reflection needs real names) -keep class com.myapp.data.model.** { *; } // Keep classes used by Retrofit (interface methods вҶ’ reflection) -keep interface com.myapp.data.api.** { *; } // Keep Parcelable implementations -keep class * implements android.os.Parcelable { *; } // Debugging R8 crashes: mapping.txt maps obfuscated вҶ’ original names // build/outputs/mapping/release/mapping.txt // Upload this to Play Console and Firebase Crashlytics!
R8 crash debugging: When you get a crash like a.b.c() line 42, use the mapping.txt file to decode it: retrace mapping.txt stacktrace.txt. Always upload mapping.txt to your crash reporting tool. Without it, release crash reports are unreadable.

Biometric Authentication

The BiometricPrompt API provides fingerprint, face, and iris authentication with a system-provided UI:

// 1. Check if biometrics are available val biometricManager = BiometricManager.from(context) when (biometricManager.canAuthenticate(BIOMETRIC_STRONG or DEVICE_CREDENTIAL)) { BiometricManager.BIOMETRIC_SUCCESS -> // ready to use BiometricManager.BIOMETRIC_ERROR_NO_HARDWARE -> // no sensor BiometricManager.BIOMETRIC_ERROR_NONE_ENROLLED -> // no biometrics set up } // 2. Build the prompt configuration val promptInfo = BiometricPrompt.PromptInfo.Builder() .setTitle("Authenticate") .setSubtitle("Verify your identity to continue") .setAllowedAuthenticators(BIOMETRIC_STRONG or DEVICE_CREDENTIAL) // If using DEVICE_CREDENTIAL, don't set negativeButtonText .build() // 3. Create BiometricPrompt with callback val biometricPrompt = BiometricPrompt( fragmentActivity, executor, object : BiometricPrompt.AuthenticationCallback() { override fun onAuthenticationSucceeded(result: AuthenticationResult) { val cryptoObject = result.cryptoObject // for crypto operations unlockSecureContent() } override fun onAuthenticationError(errorCode: Int, errString: CharSequence) { // user cancelled, lockout, etc. } override fun onAuthenticationFailed() { // biometric not recognized (user can retry) } } ) // 4. Show the prompt biometricPrompt.authenticate(promptInfo)
Authenticator LevelWhat It MeansExamples
BIOMETRIC_STRONGClass 3 biometric вҖ” hardware-verifiedFingerprint, secure face unlock
BIOMETRIC_WEAKClass 2 biometric вҖ” software-basedSome face unlock implementations
DEVICE_CREDENTIALPIN, pattern, or passwordScreen lock fallback

Runtime Permissions

Since Android 6.0 (API 23), dangerous permissions must be requested at runtime. See the Android Fundamentals module for full details. Key security considerations:

Permission best practices:
- Request permissions at the point of use, not at app launch
- Explain why you need the permission before asking (shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale)
- Handle denial gracefully вҖ” degrade functionality, don't crash
- Use the ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission() API, not the deprecated onRequestPermissionsResult

Common Android Vulnerabilities

Be aware of these attack vectors when building Android apps:

VulnerabilityWhat It IsMitigation
Intent InjectionAttacker sends crafted Intents to exported componentsSet exported=false, validate all Intent extras, use explicit Intents
SQL Injection (ContentProvider)Untrusted input in SQL queries via ContentResolverUse parameterized queries (selectionArgs), Room DAO (compile-time checked)
WebView JS Interface@JavascriptInterface exposes Java methods to loaded web contentOnly load trusted URLs, restrict exposed methods, set targetSdkVersion >= 17
Insecure BroadcastSensitive data sent via implicit broadcasts, readable by any appUse LocalBroadcastManager or explicit broadcasts with permissions
Backup Extractionandroid:allowBackup="true" lets ADB extract app dataSet android:allowBackup="false" or use android:fullBackupContent to exclude sensitive files
Clipboard LeakageOther apps can read clipboard on older APIsClear clipboard after paste, flag sensitive fields with InputType.TYPE_TEXT_VARIATION_PASSWORD
// Intent injection defense: validate before using extras class DeepLinkActivity : Activity() { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) val url = intent.getStringExtra("url") // VALIDATE вҖ” never trust external input if (url != null && url.startsWith("https://myapp.com/")) { loadUrl(url) } else { finish() // reject untrusted URLs } } } // SQL injection defense: parameterized query // BAD: val cursor = db.rawQuery("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '$input'", null) // GOOD: val cursor = db.rawQuery( "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?", arrayOf(input) // parameterized вҖ” input is escaped )
Security checklist for release:
1. R8/ProGuard enabled (isMinifyEnabled = true)
2. Cleartext traffic disabled (cleartextTrafficPermitted="false")
3. Tokens in EncryptedSharedPreferences, NOT plain prefs
4. No hardcoded API keys вҖ” use local.properties + BuildConfig
5. exported=false on all non-public components
6. android:allowBackup="false" or restricted backup content
7. Certificate pinning for sensitive API endpoints
8. mapping.txt uploaded to crash reporting dashboard

questions.title: Secure Token Storage
questions.description: Where is the most secure place to store authentication tokens on Android?
options: A text file in internal storage
A static variable in the Application class
questions.explanation: EncryptedSharedPreferences encrypts both keys and values using AES-256. Regular SharedPreferences stores plain XML. Static variables are lost on process death. For highest security, use the Android Keystore for key material.
hints: Regular SharedPreferences files are stored as plain XML

questions.title: Insecure Token Storage
questions.description: This code stores the auth token insecurely. Fix it to use encrypted storage.
questions.explanation: EncryptedSharedPreferences is a drop-in replacement encrypting both keys (AES256_SIV) and values (AES256_GCM). The MasterKey is stored in the Android Keystore (hardware-backed on most devices).
hints: There is a direct encrypted replacement in AndroidX

questions.title: Certificate Pinning
questions.description: What is certificate pinning and why would you implement it?
questions.answer: Certificate pinning validates that the server's certificate matches a known, expected certificate (or public key hash), rather than trusting any CA-signed certificate. This prevents MITM attacks where an attacker uses a rogue CA certificate to intercept HTTPS traffic.
questions.explanation: Normally, Android trusts any certificate signed by a trusted CA. Pinning adds a second check: the certificate must match a specific hash embedded in the app. Tradeoff: if the server's certificate changes, you must update the app.
hints: What if someone installs a rogue CA on the device?

questions.title: Biometric Authentication
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to set up biometric authentication.
questions.explanation: The Biometric API uses a builder for configuration, a callback for results, and authenticate() to show the system dialog. Always check BiometricManager.canAuthenticate() first.
hints: The dialog configuration uses a Builder pattern

questions.title: OAuth2 Flow
questions.description: In the OAuth2 Authorization Code flow for a mobile app, what is the correct order of steps?
options: Get token вҶ’ Redirect to provider вҶ’ Exchange code вҶ’ Use token
Redirect to auth provider вҶ’ User grants permission вҶ’ Receive auth code вҶ’ Exchange code for tokens
Send username/password directly вҶ’ Receive token вҶ’ Use token
Generate token locally вҶ’ Validate with server вҶ’ Use token
questions.explanation: The Authorization Code flow: 1) Redirect user to the auth provider's login page. 2) User authenticates and grants permission. 3) Provider redirects back to your app with an authorization code. 4) Your app exchanges the code for access and refresh tokens. Mobile apps should use PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) for additional security. Never send credentials directly вҖ” that's the Resource Owner Password flow, which is deprecated.
hints: The user authenticates with the provider, not your app directly
An intermediate "code" is exchanged for the final tokens

questions.title: Auth Interceptor
questions.description: Write an OkHttp Interceptor that adds a Bearer token from a TokenManager to every request. If the token is null, proceed without it.

checks.message: Get the original request and proceed with the chain
Add the Authorization header with Bearer token

questions.explanation: The interceptor gets the original request, checks for a token, and creates a new request with the Authorization header if a token exists. chain.proceed() sends the request down the chain. This centralizes auth logic вҖ” every API call automatically includes the token.
hints: Build a new request from the original, adding the header
chain.proceed() must always be called to continue the request
